speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 12th of June, 1996 and we're in Sydney, Australia. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, these children-- who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Paul Abram. He was maybe--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. And the other photo, he was maybe 2 and 1/2 or 3-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the photo on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the parents he was staying with--

speaker: interviewer
text: And on-- when you got him back, is that how he looked on the right? Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the people that he was staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Montens. His name was-- that time, when the picture was taken-- Paul Montens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep contact with them after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did for a while, but not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was the one on the left taken-- the photo on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '43, when he was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one on the right?

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where were those photos taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The right one was taken in Amsterdam, the left one in Blaricum. That is where he was hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, can you explain what this newspaper is?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that newspaper is the announcement of the birth of Paul, in the name of the people who looked after him during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the paper?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the newspaper? What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laarder-- that is in a little place. And Courant is newspaper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the inside of that-- the newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's a official announcement of his birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's just wait and see what it says. Can you read that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blaricum-- that is the name of the place where they lived. Born Paul-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A.J.C. Montens, and H.M. van Eijk. That was the wife's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know about this? How did you find out?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me the paper, and I had to change his name to my husband's-- my first husband's name. So I had to go through the court to get his name back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you need this article to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- to contact solicitors, and all that business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know that they were going to do this-- put this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew nothing. I just gave them the baby, and that was it. I didn't know where he went, who-- nothing-- nothing at all. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document-- what was sent to me to certify that my first husband was killed in Mauthausen, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to read it to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dutch. [SPEAKING DUTCH] [BACKGROUND NOISE] [SPEAKING DUTCH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is an [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] letter for the-- from the government to-- a death certificate from [? Bluma ?] Goldberg, who died when she was 24, in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- well, did you get these notifications on request, or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on request. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This notice is to certify that my husband was allowed to take his son back to him. So that was Karel-- from the first parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what does it say? What is the-- the top of it say there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says that he is entitled to take his son-- back.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is certificate-- my father had a business in a building, where also they were looking for criminals who did war crimes. And nobody was allowed in that building without identification. And so he had that identification to get into the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my husband, Philip Abram.

speaker: interviewee
text: That must be issued in-- oh, it was issued in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean? The-- the-- what is the card called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. That was all security in that building.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we decided to immigrate, I said to my husband, we go on the boat. And in case something happened, I think you better learn to swim. And so every morning, before he went to work, 6 o'clock, he learned to swim. It didn't make much difference if something would have happened on the boat. But he tried.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's '54. Wait a minute. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is Richard Abram. And on the right is [? Anthony ?] Abram-- two of my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they-- where were these photos taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ilana Abram and Philip Abram-- the son and the daughter of Paul Abram.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken, Selien?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape five, of the interview with Selien Abram. It's the 12th of July, 1996. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview. [INTERPOSING VOICES]
